title: French toast
subtitle: Thick bread soaked in egg and milk, fried golden in butter
excerpt: Thick slices of white bread soaked in a mixture of egg, milk, and a little cinnamon, then fried in butter until golden and slightly puffed. Served with maple syrup. Simple and good, especially with day-old bread that has dried slightly and will absorb the custard without going to mush.
sourceNotes: Egg-soaked fried bread is documented across European cooking traditions for centuries; the French 'pain perdu' (lost bread) and the English 'poor knights of Windsor' are both versions. The American name 'French toast' appears in print from the mid-nineteenth century. Fannie Farmer includes the preparation in 'The Boston Cooking-School Cook Book' (1896, Project Gutenberg #7544). Mrs Beeton's 'Book of Household Management' (1861, Project Gutenberg #10136) includes similar preparations under 'toast' variations.

content.text: Day-old bread makes better French toast than fresh. Fresh bread has a high moisture content and absorbs the custard too quickly, producing a heavy, wet centre. Bread that is a day old has dried slightly and will absorb the egg-and-milk mixture evenly through the slice rather than just on the surface. The result is a thick, custardy centre with a golden crust — exactly what the dish is meant to be.

The soaking time matters: 20 seconds per side for standard sandwich bread, up to 60 seconds for a thick slice of brioche or challah. The custard should penetrate to the middle but not over-saturate the bread to the point where it falls apart when you pick it up. Fry in a mixture of butter and a neutral oil; the oil prevents the butter from burning before the bread is cooked through.
Make the custard and soak the bread

Whisk together the {{eggs}} eggs, {{whole-milk}} of milk, {{cinnamon-ground}} of ground cinnamon, {{caster-sugar}} of sugar, and a pinch of salt in a wide, shallow bowl. Submerge each slice of bread in the custard for 20 to 30 seconds per side, pressing gently to help absorption. Lift each slice and let the excess drip off for a few seconds before cooking.

Melt the {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in the frying pan over medium heat. When the foam subsides, lay the soaked bread slices in the pan. Cook for 2 to 3 minutes without touching until golden brown on the underside. Flip and cook for a further 2 minutes until the second side matches. The bread should feel slightly firm when pressed — if it is still very soft and wobbly in the centre, give it another minute per side on a lower heat. Serve immediately with maple syrup.

items.symptom: French toast is soggy in the centre
items.cause: Bread soaked too long, or too much custard absorbed
items.fix: Limit soaking to 30 seconds per side; allow excess custard to drip off before frying

items.symptom: Outer crust is dark but centre is raw
items.fix: Cook on medium heat; lower the heat slightly after flipping if the crust is browning too fast

items.symptom: Bread falls apart in the custard
items.cause: Bread too fresh and soft, or soaked too long
items.fix: Use day-old bread; or toast the slices lightly before soaking to firm the structure

items.cause: Too little cinnamon or sugar in the mix
items.fix: Taste the custard before soaking; it should taste perceptibly of cinnamon — adjust accordingly

content.text: French toast is one of the oldest and most widely distributed egg-and-bread preparations in the world, appearing under dozens of names across dozens of cuisines. The Roman cookbook attributed to Apicius (4th–5th century, thought to be the earliest surviving Latin cookbook) contains a version. In France it is pain perdu (lost bread), a name that describes its original purpose: reviving stale bread that would otherwise be wasted. The American version is sweeter and thicker than most European versions, served as a morning dish rather than a dessert or a savoury course, and is the version that has become most widely known internationally.
